channel.title: NYT > U.S. News
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Pam Bondi Wanted a Graceful Exit. But Trump Wanted Her Gone.
item.description: Pam Bondi had a feeling her days as attorney general were numbered. But she didn’t expect President Trump to drop the curtain quite so soon.
item.dc:creator: Glenn Thrush and Tyler Pager
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: Attorney General Pam Bondi at a cabinet meeting in January. She met with President Trump on Wednesday to make her case to stay on.

item.title: Gucci Mane Held at Gunpoint, Prompting 2 Rappers’ Arrests
item.description: The hip-hop titan was forced to sign papers releasing a rapper known as Pooh Shiesty from his record contract during an armed confrontation in January at a Dallas music studio, officials said.
item.dc:creator: Neil Vigdor
item.media:credit: Paras Griffin/Getty Images
item.media:description: Gucci Mane performing in Atlanta last year.

item.title: Hegseth Allows Troops to Carry Personal Firearms at Military Bases
item.description: Many shootings at military bases have involved troops bringing guns they purchased onto base grounds and opening fire on fellow service members.
item.dc:creator: Chris Cameron
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Paul Ratje for The New York Times
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth during a visit to the U.S.-Mexico border last year.

item.title: Trump Says Opening the Strait of Hormuz ‘Should Be Easy.’ Will It?
item.description: President Trump is asking U.S. allies to “go to” the Strait of Hormuz “and just take it.” Eric Schmitt, our national security correspondent, explains how difficult it would be to clear the strait by force.
item.dc:creator: Eric Schmitt, Gilad Thaler, Christina Shaman, Laura Salaberry, Rafaela Balster, Whitney Shefte, Adina Renner and Samuel Granados

item.title: Hegseth Fires Army Chief Amid Battle With Its Leaders
item.description: Senior Army officers reacted with anger and frustration to news of Gen. Randy George’s dismissal, characterizing it as the latest blow to the service.
item.dc:creator: Greg Jaffe, Helene Cooper and Eric Schmitt
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Nick Hagen for The New York Times
item.media:description: Gen. Randy George is expected to be replaced by Gen. Christopher LaNeve, who previously served as Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth’s senior military assistant in the Pentagon.

item.title: ICE Arrests the Head of Wisconsin’s Largest Islamic Group
item.description: Officials detained Salah Sarsour, the president of the Islamic Society of Milwaukee, saying he had been convicted of crimes by Israel and had lied on a green card application in the 1990s.
item.dc:creator: Jacey Fortin
item.media:credit: via the Islamic Society of Milwaukee
item.media:description: Salah Sarsour, the president of the Islamic Society of Milwaukee, came to the United States nearly 33 years ago.

item.title: What to Know About the ‘Massive’ Military Bunker Beneath Trump’s Ballroom
item.description: President Trump has been talking about the emergency facility beneath what was once the East Wing, details of which are usually kept secret, as he tries to justify his renovation.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump shared some of his plans for the bunker, referring to the secure underground facility as a “massive complex.”

item.title: Johnson Wavers on Ending the Shutdown, Reflecting His Weak Hold on Power
item.description: The House speaker first panned, then endorsed, then punted on, then pitched and now is delaying a bill to reopen the Homeland Security Department, showing his vulnerability in the face of party rifts.
item.dc:creator: Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Speaker Mike Johnson has defined his role as a tool to further President Trump’s power and cheerlead his agenda, and in turn relied on the president to help him corral Republicans on tough votes.

item.title: The Awe of a Moon Launch in an Age of Trump, Turmoil and Tribal Divisions
item.description: The launch of Artemis II captured the tenor of the times in a country that can still do big things but seems forever mired in big problems.
item.dc:creator: Peter Baker
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: NASA’s Artemis II rocket on the launchpad at Kennedy Space Center in Cape Canaveral, Fla., on Wednesday.

item.title: How Are High California Gas Prices Affecting Your Life?
item.description: Tell us how the sharp increase in gas prices is changing how you live and work.
item.dc:creator: Jill Cowan
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Mario Tama/Getty Images
item.media:description: A gas station in Pasadena, Calif., on Monday.

item.title: Wisconsin Universities Chief Defies Board’s Push for Resignation
item.description: Jay Rothman, the president of the state university system, said he had received no explanation for why regents want to oust him.
item.dc:creator: Alan Blinder
item.media:credit: John Hart/Wisconsin State Journal, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Jay Rothman has been the president of the University of Wisconsin system since 2022.

item.title: Justice Dept. Says Trump Doesn’t Need to Hand Over Presidential Records After Office
item.description: The opinion could set the stage for President Trump to refuse to give the National Archives many of his own official documents when he leaves office.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer
item.category: Federal Criminal Case Against Trump (Documents Case)
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: A memo from the Office of Legal Counsel at the Justice Department asserts that the Presidential Records Act is unconstitutional because it is an example of lawmakers trying to wield too much authority over the president.

item.title: Who Is Todd Blanche, Trump’s Acting Attorney General?
item.description: While it remains unclear how long Todd Blanche will stay in the job, whoever ends up taking over permanently will lead a department that he has shaped in his own image.
item.dc:creator: Alan Feuer
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Todd Blanche with Pam Bondi. Mr. Blanche has enabled the wholesale politicization of the Justice Department, but also held off some of the president’s most impulsive efforts to open criminal cases unsupported by the evidence.

item.title: Trump’s Cuts Have Eviscerated Once-Bipartisan Foreign Aid Programs
item.description: Acting mostly with approval from the Republican-led Congress, President Trump clawed back money it had approved for initiatives that enjoyed strong backing from members of both parties.
item.dc:creator: Catie Edmondson
item.media:credit: Adam Dean for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rohingya refugees after crossing into Bangladesh fleeing violence in 2017.

item.title: Judge Blocks Deportation of Man Who Was Detained by ICE After Exoneration
item.description: Subramanyam Vedam was set to be freed after his murder conviction was overturned in October. The ruling that he could remain in the United States is a blow to the Trump administration.
item.dc:creator: Madeleine Ngo
item.media:credit: Geoff Rushton/StateCollege.com, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Subramanyam Vedam outside a courthouse in Pennsylvania in February. He came to the United States with his parents from India when he was 9 months old.

item.title: Planning Commission Approves Trump’s Ballroom, but Legal Roadblocks Remain
item.description: The board had been expected to vote to approve the project last month, but it was delayed after about 32,000 mostly negative comments rolled in from across the country.
item.dc:creator: Luke Broadwater
item.media:credit: Jonathan Ernst/Reuters
item.media:description: Will Scharf, who was appointed by President Trump as the chairman of the National Capital Planning Commission, at a meeting to approve the White House ballroom plans on Thursday.

item.title: Trump Says He’ll End the Iran War in Weeks. What If He Can’t?
item.description: President Trump faces the possibility that at the end of his own two-to-three week window for wrapping up the war in Iran, nothing much will have changed.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Doug Mills/The New York Times

item.title: A President, the Supreme Court and a Landmark Citizenship Order Collide
item.description: The justices seems poised to rule against the president’s birthright citizenship plan. He is already furious over their decision rejecting his tariffs program.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Outside the Supreme Court on Wednesday.

item.title: War With Iran Clarifies Trump’s Spending Priorities: The Military, Not Child Care
item.description: As the White House prepares to release its 2027 budget, President Trump said military protection, not social programs, took precedence.
item.dc:creator: Alan Rappeport
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A building in Tehran damaged by airstrikes. This week, President Trump said: “It’s not possible for us to take care of day care, Medicaid, Medicare, all of these individual things, they can do it on a state basis.”

item.title: Pam Bondi Fired as Trump’s Attorney General
item.description: In a social media post, President Trump said he was replacing Ms. Bondi with Todd Blanche, her deputy, on an interim basis.
item.dc:creator: Tyler Pager and Glenn Thrush
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Pam Bondi’s dismissal makes her the second cabinet member to lose her job in recent weeks.

item.title: In Supreme Court Birthright Citizenship Case, Trump’s Likely Loss May Not End the Fight
item.description: The justices gave respectful consideration to what was once a fringe theory and could rule against it on grounds that would allow Congress to return to the question.
item.dc:creator: Adam Liptak
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: After Supreme Court arguments on Wednesday, there were reasons to think the justices would deal President Trump’s efforts to limit birthright citizenship just a setback rather than a death blow.

item.title: Lawsuit Challenges Warrantless Searches and Forced Entries by ICE
item.description: A coalition of legal groups claims the Homeland Security Department adopted an unconstitutional policy allowing its agents to enter homes without a judicial warrant.
item.dc:creator: Zach Montague
item.media:credit: Victor J. Blue for The New York Times
item.media:description: Federal agents outside a home in Minneapolis in January, as part of President Trump’s immigration crackdown in Minnesota.

item.title: In the Birthright Citizenship Hearing, a Story of Asians Fighting for Rights
item.description: In the Supreme Court’s oral arguments, lawyers and justices cited a litany of cases reflecting how long it took for Asians to win the right to be American.
item.dc:creator: Amy Qin
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Demonstrators in support of birthright citizenship on Wednesday at the Supreme Court.

item.title: Tina Peters, Colorado Election Denier, Will Have Prison Sentence Reconsidered
item.description: Ms. Peters, a former county clerk, received a nine-year sentence after being convicted of tampering with voting machines. An appeals court overturned the sentence but did not immediately free her from prison.
item.dc:creator: Jack Healy and Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: David Zalubowski/Associated Press
item.media:description: Tina Peters, the former clerk in Mesa County, Colo., had been convicted of tampering with voting machines that were under her control.

item.title: House Punts on Homeland Security Funding, Prolonging Shutdown
item.description: Even after Speaker Mike Johnson agreed to a deal to fund the Department of Homeland Security without immigration enforcement money, the House failed to take it up amid hard-right opposition.
item.dc:creator: Carl Hulse and Michael Gold
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator John Thune of South Dakota, the majority leader, used the brief Senate session on Thursday to officially reject a House counterproposal and insist on his chamber’s compromise.

item.title: Earthquake Rattles Northern California
item.description: A 4.6-magnitude quake struck near Boulder Creek, Calif., early Thursday. But there appeared to be minimal damage.
item.dc:creator: Jill Cowan, Chris Hippensteel and Emma Schartz
item.media:credit: The New York Times

item.title: Nutrition Will Now Be Required in Medical Schools After RFK Jr. Pressure
item.description: As Robert F. Kennedy Jr. calls for medical schools to redesign curriculums, an agency that oversees dozens has deleted diversity standards and added nutrition.
item.dc:creator: Alan Blinder and Alice Callahan
item.media:credit: Pete Marovich for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mr. Kennedy announced in March that more than 50 medical schools would embrace a federal framework for nutrition education.

item.title: ActBlue May Have Misled Congress on Vetting Foreign Donations, Its Lawyers Warned
item.description: The Democratic fund-raising group is facing investigations from the Justice Department and congressional Republicans ahead of the midterm elections.
item.dc:creator: Reid J. Epstein and Shane Goldmacher
item.media:credit: Todd Heisler/The New York Times
item.media:description: Regina Wallace-Jones, the chief executive of the liberal fund-raising organization ActBlue, at the 2024 Democratic National Convention in Chicago. A 2023 letter from her to Congress later played a role in internal turmoil at ActBlue.

item.title: Supporters of Household Voting Believe U.S. Would Be Better Off Without Women’s Vote
item.description: Adherents to biblical patriarchy support household voting: One household, one vote — the husband’s. They say the idea is catching on.
item.dc:creator: Vivian Yee
item.media:credit: Adriana Zehbrauskas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Sunday church service at King’s Way Church in Prescott, Ariz.
